text: BASEL. See BASLE.
BASELICE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, 20 m. SE of Campobasso. Pop. 4,406.

BASFORD, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 2½ m. N of Nottingham. Area 2,720 acres. Pop., with the hamlet of Carrington, 8,688.—Also a township in Wybunbury p., Cheshire. Area 490 acres. Pop. 85.—Also a township in Cheddleton p., Staffordshire. Pop. 349.
BASHALL-EAVES, a township in the p. of Mitton, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 279.

BASHEE, or Bashi Islands, a group of five islands, and four rocky islets, lying to the N of Luçon, in the Philippine group, between 20° and 21° N lat. They form a long group extending N and S, and are in general high, and may be sighted at 15 leagues. They produce plantains, bananas, pineapples, sugar-canes, potatoes, yams, and cotton; and gold-dust has been collected in the beds of some of the mountain-torrents. The natives are a wellformed race of men, and of active industrious habits, employing themselves in fishing as well as cultivating the soil. The Spaniards established a settlement on these islands in 1783. The names of the five inhabited islands are Bashee, Grafton, Monmouth, Orange, and Goat island. The magnetic meridian intersects the channel between the B. islands and the Ladrones, in the meridian of 226° 48' W, in the lat. of 20° 12' N.—Beechey.
BASHEE, a river of S Africa, in Kaffraria, between the Amakosa and the Amaponda territories, flowing into the ocean in 32° 15' S lat., after a SE course of 150 m.
BASHILO, a river of Abyssinia, in Amhara, flow- BAS ing in a SW course into the Blue Nile on the 1. bank, 25 m. S of Aluta.

BASHKIRS, or BASHKURS, a numerous tribe in the Russian gov. of Orenburg. They speak the Tartar dialect, and appear to be of Turco-Mongolian origin. They are Mahommedan Sunnites in religion, and in their general habits and manners differ little from other Tartar tribes. Their principal wealth consists in their flocks, cattle, horses, and camels; they are also celebrated for their management of bees; and they cultivate rye, barley, wheat, oats, and millet. Their horses are a strong and beautiful breed. The Bashkirs form excellent horsemen, and supply the Russian army with about 3,000 cavalry, who are armed with a bow and lance, in addition to a carabine and pistol; and protected by a helmet and coat-of-mail. They are divided into 12 cantons, which are subdivided into clans and villages. Their number does not probably fall short of 500,000; as there are above 72,000 men of from 17 to 45 years of age on the military muster roll, who pay no taxes, but hold themselves in readiness for foreign service, besides defending the frontier towards the Kirghiz steppe.—Rechberg.—Pallas.—Dahl.
BASH-JELEMBE, or GELEMBEH, a large village in the sanjak of Karahsi, in Anatolia, 9 hours NE of Akhissar, in a fertile and richly-cultivated valley.
BASHLI, a town of Circassia, in Daghistan, in N lat. 42° 25′, 24 m. NW of Derbent.
BASIENTO, a river of Naples, rising in Monte Rivazzone, in the prov. of Basilicata, and flowing from W to E into the gulf of Taranto. It is the ancient Casuentus.
BASILDEN, a parish of Berkshire, 7 m. NW of Reading. Area 3,120 acres. Pop. 812.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Laindon, in Essex, 5 m. S of Billericay. Area 1,850 acres. Pop. 157.
BASILE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of La Mastre. Pop. 1,138.
BASILEUS (SAINT), or HAGIOS BASILEIOS, a village of Greece, in the prov. of Corinth, 9 m. SSW of Corinth.
BASIL HALL (PORT), a small harbour on the E coast of Tierra del Fuego, within New Year isles.
BASIL HALL BAY, an inlet on the coast of York's archipelago, in the Polar seas, in N lat. 60°, W long. 115°.—Also a bay on the SW coast of Korea, in China.

BASILICA, or Vasilika, a village of Greece, in the Morea, in the nomarchy of Corinthia, and near the l. bank of the Asopus, 9 m. NNW of Corinth. It consists of about 50 houses, and is chiefly inhabited by Albanians. It occupies the site of the ancient Sicyon; and the remains of the walls of that city are still to be seen in many parts of the circuit of the hill on which it stands, which commands a beautiful and interesting prospect.—Leake.

BASILICA'TA, a province of Naples, on the gulf of Taranto; bounded on the N by the prov. of Capitanata; on the NE by the Terra di Bari; on the E by Terra d'Otranto; on the SE by the gulf of Taranto; on the S by Calabria Citra; on the W by Principato Citra; and on the NW by Principato Ultra. From N to S, or from Melfi to the gulf of Policastro, it is 80 m. in length; its greatest breadth is 55 m. It has a square superficies of 3,134 Italian sq. m. Pop. in 1820, 418,464; in 1837, 486,270; in 1845, 481,288. The surface is generally mountainous; and the general slope, as indicated by the course of the rivers, is towards the gulf of Taranto. The main ridge of the Apennines, running along the N, sends down a number of long offsets in a SE direction, which gradually sink to the level of the plain, at the distance of about 10 m. from the sea. Between these ridges lie the basins or long narrow valleys of the Bradano, the Basiento, the Agri, and the Sinno. The soil is fertile, but badly cultivated, producing maize, wheat, oil, wine, cotton, tobacco, lint, and hemp. The vines of Tursi, Melfi, Pisticci, and Montalbano, are celebrated. The forests are more extensive in this prov. than in any other of the kingdom. Cattle, sheep, asses, mules, and pigs, are extensively reared; and silk-worms and bees form a considerable branch of rural economy. A little iron is wrought; but the mineral wealth of the district is inconsiderable. There are no manufactures of importance; and the commerce is inconsiderable. In 1834, the shipping belonging to this prov. amounted to only 23 boats of a total tonnage of 114 tons. The entire coast-line does not exceed 24 m .- This prov. is divided into four districts: viz., Potenza, Matera, Melfi, and Lagonegro; which are subdivided into 41 circondario, and 120 communi.—The present name is supposed to have been given to this region by Basileus II., on recovering it from the Saracens in the 11th cent.
BASILIO (SAN), a village of Sardinia, 24 m. N by W of Cagliari. Pop. 1,172.
BASILIPOTAMO, or VASI'LI POTAMO'. See EUROTAS.
BASILUZZO, a rocky islet in the Mediterranean, one of the Dattoli, 21 m. E of Panaria, in the Lipari group.
BASING (OLD), a parish in Hants, 2 m. NE of Basingstoke. Area 4,970 acres. Pop. 1,172.

BASINGSTOKE, a market-town and parish in Hampshire, 45½ m. SW of Nine Elms, Vauxhall bridge, London, by the Southampton and London railroad, and 7 m. W of Odiham, situated in a fertile and open part of the country, near a branch of the Loddon. It consists of several streets, containing neat and well-built houses, and possesses a markethouse and a town-hall. Druggets and shalloons are manufactured here; but the principal business carried on is a trade in corn and malt, which is facilitated by the situation of the town at the junction of five important roads, and upon the Basingstoke canal, which, navigable by vessels of nearly 50 tons burthen, extends to the river Wey, in Surrey, and thus communicates with the Thames and London. This canal was completed in 1796, at an expense of £180,000. The London and Southampton railway was completed to this place in the spring of 1839, and opened betwixt it and London on June 10th. The town was early incorporated, and sent members to parliament from the 23° of Edward I. to the 4° of Edward II., when, it is said, the privilege was withdrawn at the request of the inhabitants. Its electors now vote for the members for North-Hampshire. The revenue of the burgh in 1837 amounted to £1,288; its expenditure to nearly the same.—Several eminent men have been natives of Basingstoke. Among these are John Basing, or de Basingstoke, a celebrated Greek scholar of the 13th cent.; Sir James Lancaster, an eminent navigator in the reign of Elizabeth; and the poetical brothers, Thomas and Joseph Warton. Pop. in 1801, 2,589; in 1831, 3,581; in 1841, 4,066.
BASINGTHORPE, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. NW of Corby. Area 920 acres. Pop., with Westby hamlet, 137.
BASIN HARBOUR, an important port on the E side of Lake Champlain, in Addison co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., through which a considerable amount of the exports and imports of that region pass.
BASKERVILLE (CAPE), a headland of Australia, on the coast of Dampier's land, in S lat. 17°, E long. 122° 28'. The land over it rises to an elevation of nearly 200 ft.

BASKINRIDGE, a township in Somerset co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 46 m. N by E of Trenton. BASLE, BASEL, or BALE, a small canton-or rather two cantons—in the NW of Switzerland, lying between the Rhine and the Jura, and the parallels of 47° 20' and 47° 37' N; and bounded on the N by France and the grand duchy of Baden; on the E by the cant. of Aargau; on the S by the cant. of Soleure; and on the W by Soleure and Berne. It BAS is about 23 m. in length from NW to SE; and nowhere exceeds 14 m. in breadth; while towards the middle it is contracted to about 3 m. Its superficial area is stated in the Almanach de Gotha for 1849, at 8.71; by Möller at 8; and by Ungewitter at 9 German sq. m. We may perhaps take it at 8.5 German, or 180.5 English sq. m. The Jura and its offsets intersect the E part of the cant., the Hauenstein and the Schaffmatt rising on its S frontier, the former to an alt. of 3,000 ft., the latter to 4,000 ft. The N districts slope towards the Rhine, and are watered by various streams, which cross the country generally in a NW direction from the high lands on the E and S towards that great river. The river next in importance to the Rhine is the Birse, which flows into the Rhine half-a-mile to the E of the city of Basle.— The climate is temperate and salubrious; and the lower hills are generally clothed to the top with vines or with luxuriant pasture. Vegetables and fruit are plentiful and cheap; but wood is scarce. Wine is made of pretty good quality; and about 12,000 head of large cattle are reared. The extent of arable land was estimated in 1829 at 32,500 acres. The richest districts lie along the Rhine, and in the valleys of the Birse and the Ergolz. Coal and iron exist in the district. The pop. in 1774 was estimated at 38,500; in 1837, it amounted to 55,500, of whom 6,000 were Catholics, and 49,500 Protestants. About one-third of the pop. are engaged in different branches of manufacturing industry; and for two centuries that of ribbons has been the principal manufacture. In 1754, the number of looms in the cant. was estimated at 1,238, of which 837 belonged to the town of B. In 1836, there were about 4,000 looms in the town, producing ribbons plain and figured, taffetas, and a few satins. The exports of ribbons in 1834 from this cant. were valued at about 10,000,000 francs, equal to two-sevenths of that of the whole exportation of France in that year. About one-half of this export goes to the United States; the other half to Germany, France, Holland, Denmark, and Sweden. A large proportion of the weavers employed in producing this amount of ribbons live in the country, and even in the adjacent cantons of Berne and Aargau. Almost all these workmen have some small landed property, and labour alternately as agriculturists and manufacturers. Other branches of industry, once flourishing, as the printing of cotton goods, finishing woollen cloths, making of paper, leather, &c., have fallen off of late years. [Bowring.] The cant. of B. is divided into seven districts, each of which is governed by an obervogt or baillie, appointed by the two councils of the state. The town of B., with the districts lying on the N of the Rhine, form a canton of themselves, distinct from the country section, which is composed of the other five districts. Each of these divisions has its own grand and petty council, and its own president; but the two have only a vote between them in the Helvetic diet. It is understood that the majority of the pop. of the town division are aristocratical in their political tendencies; while that of the country division are democratical. The manufacturers, capitalists, and traders, almost exclusively belong to the former party; the agriculturists to the latter. The public revenue before the separation of the two parties amounted to about £28,000. The federal contingent is 918 men; and of the pecuniary contingent of 22,950 francs, the town canton pays 14,145 francs; the country, 8,805 francs.—The language is a dialect of the Swiss-German.—Bale-Campagne, or Basel- Landschaft, is divided into the bailiwicks of Liesthal, Waldenburg, Sissach, and Arlesheim; which are subdivided into 74 communes. Its pop. in 1837 was 41,103. Its cap. is Liesthal. Bale-Ville, or Basel- Stadttheil, comprises the city of B, and its banlieue or suburbs, and the three rural communes of the ancient cant. of B., situated on the r. bank of the Rhine, viz., Riehen, Bettingen, and Klein-Huningen. It had a pop. in 1837 of 24,321. History.] In the middle ages, this cant. formed part of the Burgundian empire. In later times it was governed by an imperial bailiff, and by the bishop of B., who was a prince of the empire. In 1501, it was admitted a member of the confederacy. It was the first canton which separated from the Helvetic confederacy, and adopted the new constitution fabricated for Switzerland by the French allies. The progress of the revolution was here almost instantaneous. The peasants, always jealous of the monopolies of privileges vested in the burghers, and now urged on by the artful intrigues of the French, rose in different districts, demolished the castles of the bailiffs, and loudly demanded emancipation and independence. The magistrates, overawed by the enemy's army, which had already overrun the bishopric, and threatened their capital, were obliged to submit without a struggle. On the 24th of January, 1798, the tree of liberty was planted on the walls of the city of B.; and on the 5th of Feb., the magistrates resigned their authority, and 60 delegates, appointed by the people, were invested with temporary power, till the new constitution should be properly organized. With the addition of the lower part of the Frickthal, B, formed one of the departments into which Switzerland was divided by the constitution of the 29th of May 1801, with the right of sending three representatives to the diet. From 1806 to 1812 the assemblies of the federal diet were held in the town of B. The hereditary fends of town and country, again burst forth with such violence after the revolution of 1830, that a fierce conflict actually took place between the two parties; and the Swiss diet were compelled to pass an act for the formal separation of this petty state, in Aug. 1833, into two distinct and independent republics, with one federal vote between them, which they have hitherto managed to fling away, as the two parties invariably spend their half-vote in neutralizing that of each other. Basle, or Basil, the cap. of the above subdivided canton, is beautifully situated on the banks of the Rhine, about 60 m. S of Strasburg, and 120 m. NE of Geneva, in N lat. 47° 33′ 37", E long. 7° 39', at an alt. of 800 ft. above sea-level. The river, - which is here in its greatest beauty, broad, deep, and rapid,—divides the city into two parts, called the Large and the Small town, which are joined by a noble bridge of 14 arches, 600 ft. in length. The larger portion lies on the l. bank. Each of these divisions is surrounded with walls and a ditch; and the ancient defences of the town remain in a good state of preservation. Without the compass of the Large town, there are extensive suburbs, which form so many distinct quarters, and are enclosed by a regular rampart. B. is the largest, and was once the most populous town in Switzerland. It is capable of containing upwards of 100,000 inhabitants, though its present pop. does not exceed 20,500. Its great manufacture of ribbons has been already noticed; among its other manufactures are those of tobacco, paper, gloves, turnery. It also forms an important entrepot between France, Germany, the Netherlands, and Switzerland. No place can boast of a greater number of fountains, some of which have even their source within the town; besides the Birs, a stream which falls into the Rhine, a little above the city, supplies it by means of a canal with water, particularly well adapted to various purposes of trade. Its houses are in general built of stone, in a neat and elegant taste; but except some splendid mansions, in which a few rich manufacturers display their wealth, there are no buildings whose magnificence can offend the republican spirit of its neighbours. There are no fewer than 53 public fountains in the city; that in the fishmarket is one of the finest of its class. The cathedral, on the l. bank of the river, is a superb Gothic structure, with two steeples each 205 ft. high, but much disfigured by the rose-coloured paint with which it is bedaubed. It contains the monuments of many illustrious persons, and is particularly consecrated by that of the great Erasmus, who made this town his principal residence, and published here many of his valuable works. The Pfalz or terrace BAS of the cathedral, which serves as a public promenade, commands a rich and extensive view. Besides the cathedral, B. contains 6 parochial churches, and seven convents, which were secularized by the Reformation. The public library contains 50,000 volumes; but is more remarkable for the rare and valuable editions of the books which it contains, than for the number of its volumes: it is enriched with numerous manuscripts, the most curious of which are the letters of the first reformers, and of other learned men in the 15th, 16th, and 17th centuries. There is a suite of apartments connected with the library, which contain a cabinet of petrifactions, some ancient medals and gems, a few antiquities found at Augst, a large collection of prints, and some admirable drawings and paintings, consisting chiefly of originals by Holbein, who, it is contended by some, was a native of B., and the favourite painter of Henry VIII., to whom he was introduced by Erasmus. - In these paintings-which are in high preservation-the progress of Holbein may be traced, from the earliest efforts of his pencil till he attained that perfection in the art for which he has been so generally admired. 'The Dance of Death' on the walls of an ancient convent of Dominicans, pointed out to strangers as a production of Holbein's, has been proved, from incontestable authority, to have been painted before he was born. The hall still remains in which were conducted the deliberations of the famous council of B., which, after sitting for many years, came to the resolution of deposing the pope; and published many edicts for the reformation of the church. Basle is the seat of an university, which once ranked among the most eminent seminaries of learning in Europe. It was founded in 1460 by Pope Pius II., and reorganized in 1817; and its fame will be perpetuated in literary history by the illustrious names of Oecolampadius, Amerbach, the three Bauhins, Grynaus, Buxtorf, Wetstein, Iselin, the Bernouillis, and Euler .- There are also a normal school for training teachers, a polytechnic school, an agricultural school, and a theological institute with a library of 20,000 vols. The bishopric of B., suppressed in 1792, was reestablished in 1828.—Steamers ply daily between B. and Strasburg; and there is a railway between the two cities, by way of Colmar and Mülhausen,-The mean annual temp. at B. is 49° 30'; that of January 28° 30′; of July 66° 30′.
BASLOW, a chapelry in the p, of Bakewell, Derbyshire, 3½ m. NE of Bakewell. Area 2,360 acres. Pop. 962.
BASMAN, or Bomosa, an island in the Persian gulf, in N lat. 25° 48', E long. 55° 10'. It is 25 m. from the main, and is about 16 m. in circumf.
BASON RIVER, a small stream flowing into the N shore of the gulf of St. Lawrence, 11 m. E of Cape Cormorant.
BASOVIZZA, a town of Austria, in Illyria, in the gov. of Trieste, 4 m. E of Trieste.
BASQUE CAPE, a mountainous headland on the N shore of the estuary of the St. Lawrence, to the SW of the mouth of the Saguenay river.
BASQUE ISLAND, a small narrow isle, off the S shore of the gulf of St. Lawrence, 20 m. from the W end of Isle Bic.
BASQUE ISLANDS (GREAT and LITTLE), two islets in Seven Islands bay, on the N shore of the gulf of St. Lawrence.

BASQUE (PAYS), a district of France, in Gascony, on the Spanish frontier, chiefly comprised in the modern arrondissements of Bayonne and Mauléon, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées. The territories called Les Basques, or Les Pays des Basques, consisted of three divisions or districts: viz., Le Labourdan, or, in Basque, Laphurduy; La Soule, or, in Basque, Zuberna; and La Basse Navarre, or the Basquish Garrazi. The river Adour bounds these territories on the N; on the E is the county of Béarn; on the S, are the Pyrenees; and on the W, the Bidassoa, and the ocean. The area of the whole mountainous region within these boundaries is about 2,000 sq. m.
BASQUE (Port Aux), a small but good harbour on the S coast of the gulf of St. Lawrence, 6 m. E of Cape Ray, the SW extremity of Newfoundland.

BASQUE PROVINCES, or VASCONGA'DAS PROVI'NCIAS, the general name by which the three Spanish provinces of Alava, Biscaya, and Guipuzcoa are usually known. These provinces, in common with the Pays Basque of France, are inhabited by a race of people called Basques, Basks, Vascones, or Biscayans, the descendants, it is generally supposed, of the ancient Iberi, who, in spite of Roman, Moorish, and Gothic conquest, have maintained their language, manners, institutions, and to a great extent physical characteristics, throughout all the phases of European history. The name Basque is derived by some from bassoco, 'a mountaineer.' Humboldt derives it from basoa, 'a forest;' whence baso-coa, 'belonging to a forest,' or 'a forester.'
BASQUEVILLE. See BACQUEVILLE.
BASRADU, a town of Western Africa, in the kingdom of Bambarra, on the l. bank of the Joliba, 8 m. SE of Bammaku.

BASS (THE), a stupendous insulated rock in the frith of Forth, off the coast of East Lothian, 32 m. ENE from North Berwick, and 12 m. N from the nearest point of the coast, in N lat. 56° 4′ 53", W long. 2° 37′ 57". It is of a circular form, about the sixth of a mile in diam., and 400 ft. high. Some parts, not less than from 200 to 300 ft. in height, project in lofty precipices over the sea in the most terrific form; and, rising gradually, the summit resembles an obtuse cone. The continual beating of the waves has opened vast excavations all around the lower sides. The sea is of great depth on the N, E, and W, but shallow on the S, to which point also the rock declines, and here it is accessible in calm weather. A cavern runs through this rock from NW to SE. It is quite dark in the centre, where there is a deep pool of water, but widens towards both apertures; that on the SE side is the highest. There is a spring of water on the rock, and grass for a few sheep. Various corallines, and fuci, are produced in the surrounding sea. The Bass is chiefly remarkable for vast quantities of solan geese, which resort to it in March for the purpose of breeding, and depart in September. —There was formerly a castle on this islet, which was afterwards converted into a state-prison. After the Revolution a party of the adherents of James having obtained possession of it, committed piracies by means of a boat, and held out here after all the rest of Britain had surrendered. But their boat being at length either seized or lost, they were compelled to capitulate. This rock is a very conspicuous object, and visible from a considerable distance, The tide ebbs 19 ft. at the B.
BASS HARBOUR, a harbour in the Eastern seas, formed by several small islands off the coast of Malacca, 45 m. W of Queda.
BASS ISLANDS, a group in Lake Erie, in the state of Ohio, U. S., 4 m. N of Sundusky.
BASS RIVER, a river of East Greenland, which runs into the sea in W long. 50° 10′, N lat. 64° 30′.

BASS STRAIT, a channel in the South Pacific ocean, separating New Holland from Van Diemen's land. It is somewhat less than 50 leagues in length from E to W, and 120 m. in breadth from N to S, and affords an open and safe passage for vessels navigating to and from Port Jackson. Several groups BAS of islands, chiefly barren and unproductive, and among some parts of which the navigation is dangerous, lie within the straits. The W entrance, formed by the islands off the NW point of Van Diemen's land, and Cape Otway on the Australian continent, is 108 m. wide. These islands are probably the remains of land once continuous between the two shores. [Mitchell.] All the earlier navigators supposed Van Diemen's land an integral portion of the vast continent of New Holland, until Mr. Bass, a surgeon of a man-of-war, conceived there were indications of a strait in about 40° S lat. A subsequent voyage proved that his conjecture was not erroneous; and the channel thus discovered received his name. January and February are the best months for making a passage to the westward through Bass strait. - Flinders. - Stokes.

BASSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, and cant. of Jarnac. It produces wine, and has extensive distilleries of brandy. In the vicinity of the village a bloody conflict took place between the Catholics and Protestants in 1569. Pop. 806. BASSA - DOUNGRAM - OOLA, [i. e. 'Yak or Bull mountain,' so called from its configuration, a lofty snow-capped mountain-chain in Tibet, in N lat. 34° 30′, E long. 91° 50′. It forms the southern side of the basin of the Katsi-oulan-mouren, the principal head-stream of the Mouroni-oussou.
BASSAIN. See Bassein.
BASSALAM. See ANGRAB.
BASSALEG, a parish in Monmouthshire, 3 m. W of Newport. Area 6,290 acres. Pop. 1,731.

BASSAM (GRAND), a town of W Africa, the cap. of a small state in the territory of Ashantee, situated at the embouchure of the Sueiro da Costa, and 115 m. WNW of Cape Three Points. Gold forms its chief article of export. The French journals report [1849] that the river of Grand B. has been found to have a navigable connexion with the Niger.
BASSAM PICCANINNY, a town of W Africa, in the Quaquas territory, on the Ivory coast, 20 m. WNW of Grand Bassam.
BASSAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Beziers. Pop. 440.
BASSAN (EL), a town of Albania, near the r. bank of the Scombi, 27 m. above the entrance of that river into the Adriatic, and 25 m. NNE of Herat.

BASSANO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 20 m. NNE of Vicenza, on the Brenta, 25 m. NNW of Padua. It is substantially built of stone, and surrounded by walls. The footpaths are formed of marble from the adjacent mountains, and the streets are well-made, excellent paving materials being supplied by the blocks of granite, porphyry, schist and lava which abound in the environs. It possesses an ancient castle fortified by the tyrant Ezzelino, extensive barracks, a gymnasium, 4 convents, an hospital, &c. The Brenta is here crossed by a handsome stone bridge 180 ft. in length. The principal manufactures of the town are cloth, paper, straw bonnets, and copper utensils. There are also silk-spinning-mills, and wax-bleacheries, and the printing establishment of Remondini was once one of the first in Italy. Its commerce consists chiefly in silk, common cloth, timber, charcoal, grain, wine, cattle, and iron. The climate of B. is pure and bracing. Pop. in 1833, 10,356.
BASSAR PULO, an islet of the Asiatic archipelago, near the N coast of the island of Ceram, in S lat. 2° 45′, and E long. 129° 10′.

BASSAS, a native tribe of Western Africa, who occupy the coast to the SE of the colony of Liberia, along an extent of 180 m. Their numbers are estimated at 125,000. They are a temperate and industrious race, and export immense quantities of cattle, rice, and palm-oil. They occupy villages of from 40 to 2,000 inhabitants, and never at a great distance from the coast.
BASSAS, or BAXAS CAPE, a headland of E Africa, | 525 m. SSW of Cape Guardafui, in N lat. 4° 55'.

BASSAS DA INDIA, a group near the southern entrance of the channel of Mozambique, in S lat. 21° 30', E long. 39° 38', 180 m. W of Madagascar. The largest of the group, called Europa island-which Owen thinks may be the Juan de Lisboa of early navigators—is 7 m. in circumf., and is covered with brushwood, and said to be full of wild hogs.
BASSEBERG. See SEBASTIANBERG.
BASSECOURT, or ALTORF, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, on the r. bank of the Sorne, 10 m. SE of Porrentruy. It has extensive paper manufactories. Pop. 806, Catholic.

BASSEE (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, and arrond. of Lille. The canton comprises 11 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 13,497. The town is situated on the canal of the same name, 15 m. SW of Lille. It has extensive manufactories of oil, sugar from beet-root, chicory, brandy, pottery, bricks, tiles, hosiery, soap, a cotton-spinning mill, &c. Its trade-which from the advantages of its local position is extremely active—consists chiefly in coals, grain, lint, and linen fabrics. General fairs are held four times a-year, and linen fairs on a large scale every month. It was formerly a place of considerable strength, and sustained several sieges. It was taken from the Spaniards by the French in 1646, and dismantled in 1667. Pop. 2,480.—The canal of Bassee extends from the town of the same name to the ferry of Beauvin, a distance of nearly 5 m., and is connected with a series of canals and rivers which complete the communication with the coast. Coal is its principal article of transit.

BASSEELAN, or Basilan, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Sulu sea, near the SW extremity of the island of Mindanao. It is about 40 m. in length, and 6 in average breadth. It rises in the centre to a considerable height, but its coasts are low, and afford no good harbours. The soil is generally fertile, producing grain, sugar-cane, and bananas. Deer and wild boars are common, and its shores abound with cowries. The pop. is scanty. The sovreignty of the island is claimed by the Sulus.

BASSEIN, a seaport town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, on the N side of the embouchure of the Koolaus, 27 m. N of Bombay, in N lat. 19° 20', E long. 72° 50. This town is of considerable size, and its fortifications both strong and extensive; but it is said to be becoming rapidly depopulated. Its inhabitants were chiefly Roman Catholics. The surrounding district, although marshy, is generally well-cultivated; and to the N and NE, along the Ghauts, are the extensive teak-forests which supply the dock-yard of Bombay.—The town of B. was taken and fortified by the Portuguese in 1531, and in 1750 it was occupied by the Mahrattas. In 1802 it fell into the hands of the English; and on the 31st December of the same year the famous treaty was concluded which annihilated the confederation of the Mahrattas.

BASSEIN, or Bassain, a district, town, and river of Burmah, in the prov. of Pegu. The district is bounded on the N by the Pashim nullah or creek, which falls into the Irawaddi; on the E by the Irawaddi, which divides it from the prov. of Dallah; on the S by the ocean; and on the W by a range of mountains running parallel to the coast, which separate it from Gna-Giung, a district which has of late years, however, been added to the B. district. The superficial area, including Gna-Giung, is about 9,000 sq. m. The surface is low and flat, and overrun with jungle and forest. It is watered by the two great branches into which the Irawaddi divides above BAS Henzada, the most westerly of which, known as the B. river, falls into the sea at Negrais, and is navigable for 50 m. In the dry season, however, the flow of water into it from the Irawaddi is interrupted or even cut-off by sand-banks. Towards the end of the rains, the whole country is generally under water for some days; and the surface everywhere presents numerous small lakes. The climate is temperate; the excessive heat being moderated by the sea-breeze. Rice, maize, and yams, are extensively cultivated; tobacco and the sugar-cane, to a limited extent. Indigo and cotton grow freely in the upper parts of the prov., and sesame and the Palma christi are cultivated in the gardens. Palms are not numerous; but the mango and the jack-tree abound. Silk and cotton goods, common earthenware, and iron implements, form the leading articles of manufacture. The trade of the country was at one period considerable, with Rangoon, Chittagong, and even Dacca. The articles of export were rice, salt, balachong, and salted and dried fish. The prov. is divided into townships, which are subdivided into districts, and each district is divided into villages. A sugi is at the head of each village-division; and a myosugi at the head of each township. The pop. is scanty, especially in the lower districts: over the whole prov. it does not exceed 24 to the sq. m. It consists of Burmese, Taliens, Karians, and Kyens.—The town of B. is situated on the Bassain, or western arm of the Irawaddi, 90 m. W of Rangoon, and is one of the three principal ports of the kingdom. Its communication with the central Irawaddi is however, as above stated, limited to flood-seasons. Its pop. was at one time estimated at 30,000; but in 1826 at only 8,000.
BASSEN, a town of Hanover, 10 m. E of Bremen.
BASSENGE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 6 m. SW of Maestricht. It has extensive manufactories of straw-hats. Pop. 711.
BASSENS, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and cant. of Carbon Blanc. It affords excellent red wines. Pop. 94.

BASSENTHWAITE, a parish and v. in Cumberland, 5 m. NW of Keswick. Area of p. 6,930 acres. Pop. 536. The picturesque vale of B. stretches along the E side of the lofty Skiddaw, and contains a beautiful lake, about 4 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth.
BASSERSTORF, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Zurich, and midway between Zurich and Winterthur.

BASSE-TERRE, the capital of the island of St. Christopher's, in the Antilles. It is situated on the SW coast, in a small bay at the embouchure of a river of the same name, and possesses an active commerce. Pop. 8,000. The vale of B., in softness, richness, and perfection of cultivation, is almost unrivalled, even in W Indian scenery.

BASSE-TERRE (LA), the capital of the island of Guadaloupe, in the French Antilles, situated on the SW coast, at the embouchure of the Rivière-aux- Herbes, in N lat. 15° 59′ 30″, W long. 61° 45′. It is defended by several batteries, and, landwards, by the fort Richepense, which commands the adjacent mountains. It consists of two parochial subdivisions, named Notre-Dame-du-Mont-Carmel, and St. Francois, separated by the Herbes; and contains two parish-churches, an arsenal, a hall of justice, a handsome government-house, a large hospital, &c. It has also a botanic garden, and many beautiful and wellpaved promenades. The river, which presents a nearly dry channel during nine months of the year, is crossed by two bridges, one substantially built of stone. Numerous public fountains afford the town an ample supply of water. The roadstead is altogether destitute of shelter, and tornadoes are of frequent occurrence. Pop. 9,000, of whom 4,000 were slaves. The district of Basse-Terre is watered by the | invaded by the Persians. After its reconquest, it was river of the Galions, and produces sugar-cane, coffee, cotton, and manioc.—B. was founded in 1635. In 1691, and twice in the subsequent century, it was sacked by the English. In 1782 it was to a great extent destroyed by fire.
BASSETLAW, a large division of the co. of Nottingham, of which the borough of East Retford forms the centre. Area 197,021 acres; and is subdivided into Hatfield, North Clay, and South Clay divisions.
BASSEVELDE, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of E Flanders, 12 m. N of Ghent.

BASSIGNANA, a town and district of Piedmont, in the prov. and 9 m. NE of Alexandria, on the r. bank of the Po, and near the confluence of the Tanaro with that river. It was formerly fortified. Pop. 4,000.
BASSIGNY, a small district of France, in the ancient prov. of Champagne, of which the principal town was Chaumont.
BASSILAN. See Basseelan.
BASSINGBOURNE, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 3 m. NW of Royston. Area 4,235 acres. Pop. 1,774, of whom 191 were in the hamlet of Kneesworth.
BASSINGHAM, a parish in Lincoln, 8 m. NE of Newark. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 792.
BASSIS, an island of W Africa, on the coast of Senegambia, in the delta of the Geba or Bissao.
BASSON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, and cant. of Ivigny. It is situated on the Yonne, and possesses a small port. Wine forms its chief article of export. Pop. 638.
BASSONES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gers, and cant. of Montesquion, 10 m. NW of Mirande. It has some acidulous mineral springs. Pop. 1,667.

BASSORA, or Bassra, a mutsellimlick, or subdivision, of the pashalik of Bagdad, in Asiatic Turkey, situated between N lat. 29° 50' and 32° 5', and E long. 43° 45' and 49° 10'. It is bounded by Persia on the NE; on the SE by the Persian gulf; on the SW by the Arabian desert; and on the W by the pash. of Bagdad. Except in the western part, where some hillocks appear, the whole country is a continuous flat, and so low that the tide is felt 140 m. from the sea. The Tigris, the Euphrates, and the Shatal-Arab—which the two rivers form by their junction near the centre of the district - intersect it near the centre, and frequently cover it with their waters; but instead of digging new canals, in order to conduct to a distance the waters of the Shatal-Arab, and give greater facility to the draining-off the inundations, the inhabitants have quite neglected the old canals, most of which are now of little use. The result is, that a great part of the soil, which is formed of the continual accumulations of earth brought down by the two large rivers, is washed away, and much of the surface presents only an arid desert or infected marshes. Happily the SE winds, which blow almost without interruption, refresh the atmosphere, and oppose themselves to the ravages of the pestilential miasmata with which it is charged; and the influence of these winds is favourable both to men and vegetables. The country is covered with date-trees, on the fruit of which the Arab population chiefly subsist, to the neglect of husbandry. The fellahs, or peasants, cultivate grain, melons, and a little cotton and tobacco. The Bedouins rear camels, horses, sheep, and goats. Wild animals are few in number, and these are boars, lions, and other carnivorous beasts. Fish abounds in the Shat-al- Arab. The district contains about 150,000 inhabitants, almost all Arabs. It is divided into 10 sanjaks.—The pashalik of B. was governed by its own pasha till the middle of the 18th cent., when it was of some of the canals which had been suffered to fall BAS placed by the Porte under the pasha of Bagdad, who administered the government by a mutsellim. For its defence against the Wahabites and other hostile hordes, a rampart or wall was raised on the side of Arabia. Bassora, Balsora, Basrah, or Basra, the cap. of the above district, is situated on the r. bank of the Shat-al-Arab, 60 m. to the NW of its entrance into the Persian gulf, 210 m. S of Ispahan, 250 m. SE of Bagdad, and 1,815 m. SE of Constantinople; in N lat. 30° 29′ 30", E long. 47° 39'. It is surrounded by walls and ditches, the former 20 ft. in thickness, and planted with cannon, and comprising an area 10 m. in circuit, covered to a great extent with gardens and date-plantations, and intersected by numerous canals communicating with the river and cleansed by the tide twice every day. The situation of the town is unhealthy, owing to the inundations of the river, from which noxious exhalations arise, and strangers are in consequence liable to be attacked by fever after a short residence; but the environs are fertile, abounding in fruit and grain, and whole fields are cultivated with roses for the purpose of distillation. The town is ill-built, and totally destitute of architectural adornment. The houses consist of earth or brick, and the streets are narrow, sinuous, and excessively dirty. The bazaars are only remarkable for their extent and the richness of the commodities with which they are stored. The port of B. is the most important in the Persian gulf. The river admits vessels of 500 tons burden, and the advantages of its relative position render it the great commercial entrepot of Turkey, Persia, India, and Eastern Asia. The principal exports are dates of local produce, attar of roses, and horses. The chief articles of the transit-trade are gold, silver, copper, gall-nuts, raw silk, and dried fruits, muslin, shawls, silk, paper, porcelain, dyes, lacquer, spices, drugs, rice, coffee and sugar from India and Eastern Asia; shawls, assafætida, fruits, and horses, from Persia; horses, coffee, dates, and gums, from Arabia; pearls from Bahrein; coral from the Mediterranean; wrought metals, lead, iron, steel, and tin, and fabrics of cotton and woollen manufacture, from Europe. The English, French, and some other European nations, have factories at B.; but the maritime commerce is almost exclusively confined to the former, and to the Arabs chiefly of Muskat. The land-trade is carried on by caravans to Aleppo, Damascus, Bagdad, and other towns in the interior. - The Arab tribes of Zibes and Munlefiks receive an annual subsidy of 100,000 piastres for the defence, or rather non-plundering, of the city. The pop., consisting of Arabs, who form about a half of its number, -of Persians, who constitute a fourth, -of Turks, Armenians, Jews, Kurds, Europeans, and Hindus,amounts to 60,000. Among the Arabs are found some wealthy individuals; but the majority are extremely poor, and labour hard for small wages, on which the simplicity of their fare, consisting chiefly of a few dates with bread and water, enables them to subsist. Such of the Turks as are resident here generally hold offices under government, either civil or military; and the class of merchants is generally composed of Armenians, many of whom have attained to great consideration in the place. The surrounding district, except in the lower grounds-which are rendered barren by the periodical inundations of the brackish waters of the river-is extremely fertile, and generally well-cultivated, producing dates of the finest description, and other varieties of fruit in great luxuriance, grain, and-in small quantities-cotton and tobacco. B. has of late years been materially improved, especially in salubrity, by the reconstruction into disrepair.—This town was founded in the year 635, during the califat of Omar, and soon became one of the most flourishing in the East. It was taken, after a series of assaults, by the Turks, in 1668. In 1777 it was regained for a brief space by the Persians, and in 1787 was occupied for a term still briefer by the Arabs.

BASSUM, a town of Hanover, in the county and 16 m. W of Hoya. A Lutheran chapter of noble ladies exists here, in an abbey founded in the 9th century. Straw-hats are the principal article of maanufacture in B. Pop. 1,609. BASSUM, BAUSIM, or WAUSIM, a district of Hindostan, in the Deccan, between the 20th and 21st parallels. Its surface is hilly, and it is intersected by several of the tributaries of the Payn Gunga. Its chief town, of the same name, is on the road from Hyderabad to Nagpur, 110 m. E of Aurungabad.
BASTA, a town of Anatolia, in the sanj. of Boli, on the r. bank of the Filbos, 15 m. above the entrance of that river into the Black sea, and 5 m. above the confluence of the river of the same name.

BASTA (Tel), or Beshbesh, a town of Lower Egypt, in the prov. of Sharkieh, near the l. bank of the canal of Moes, 10 m. N of Belbeis. It is on the supposed site of the ancient Bubastus, and contains ruins of great extent.

BASTAD, or BATSTA, a town of Sweden, in the prov. and 60 m. NW of Christianstadt. It is situated on the gulf of Laholm, in the Kattegat, and possesses a small port and a custom-house. The commerce of the town is very limited. Fishing forms one of the chief industrial occupations of its inhabitants.

BASTAN, or BAZTAN (VAL DE), an administrative subdivision of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, situated on the southern declivity of the Pyrenees, enclosed by lofty mountains, and terminating on the N in the French dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées. It is 30 m. in length, and 15 in average breadth, and is watered by two streams which fall into the Bidassoa. It produces corn, maize, lint, and fruits; but the numerous herds of cattle which cover its luxuriant meadows form its staple wealth. It contains 14 villages, of which Elizondo is the chief. The valley is governed by an alcade, elected triennially. A large majority of the pop., which amounts to 8,000, have been ennobled for services rendered to the crown of Spain.

BASTAN, a village of Anatolia, in the sanj. of Boli, near the r. bank of the Sakaria, 15 m. above the entrance of that river into the Black sea. It occupies the site of the ancient Bithynium, Claudiopolis, or Antinopolis.
BASTARD, a township of Upper Canada, in the co. of Leeds, lying along the S bank of the Rideau lake. Pop. in 1842, 3,058.
BASTATAL, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, near the E coast of the island of Sumatra, in the delta of the Indragiri, in S lat. 0° 33'.

BASTELICA, a canton and commune in the island of Corsica, arrond. of Ajaccio. The cant. comprises 5 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 4,317. The town is situated in a hilly district, near the r. bank of the Prunetta, 16 m. ENE of Ajaccio. Pop. 2,314.

BASTENNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, and cant. of Amou, situated on a height bathed by the Luy, 15 m. ENE of Dax, and 5 m. NNW of Amou. Its produce in white wines is considerable and of good quality. It contains a cold mineral spring, similar to that of Barreges. Beautiful crystals of arragonite are found in the locality.

BASTIA, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of the island of Corsica.-The arrond. comprises a superficial area of 136,209 hectares, and contains 19 cant., viz., Bastia, Borgo, Brando, Campile, | Pop. 2,003. BAS Campitello, Cervione, St. Florent, Lama, Luri, San- Martino-di-Lota, Murato, San-Nicolao, Nonza, Oletta, Pero, Santo-Pietro, La Porta, Rogliano, and Vescovato. Its pop. in 1831 was 56,649; in 1836 it amounted to 63,764.—The cant. contains only the com. of the same name.—The town of B., consisting of two districts, the Terra Vecchia and the Terra Nuova, is prettily situated on a small bay, at the base of a lofty hill on the NE coast, 65 m. NNE of Ajaccio, in N lat. 42° 43', E long. 9° 26'. It is defended by a citadel of considerable strength, and by a series of minor fortifications, but its suburbs extend considerably beyond the walls. The houses are lofty, but ill-built, and the streets narrow and tortuous. The principal public buildings are the cathedral, and the chapel of the Santa Croce, a new and remarkably elegant edifice. There are also a royal college, a royal hydrographical school, a public library, and a society for the diffusion of useful knowledge. The port is small, and incapable of affording security to vessels of large dimensions. Good anchorage in 10 or 12 fathoms is however to be found a little to the E of the Mole. The entrance to the port, which is only 90 yards in width, is partly defended on the NE by an isolated rock of calcareous structure, called, from the remarkable similarity of its configuration to the form of that animal, Il Leone. The building-docks are of some extent, and B. possesses also manufactories of soap, leather, wax, liquorice, &c., and 2 printing establishments. Its commerce consists of grain, oil, wine, citron, skins, oak-bark, goats' hair, coral and leather, which are exported chiefly to France and Italy. In 1839 the shipping which entered and left the port amounted to 16,769 tons. Steam-boats ply regularly once a-week between this port and Toulon. In 1745 B. was bombarded and taken from the Genevese by the English, but was restored the same year. It sustained an ineffectual siege by the Austrians and Piedmontese in 1748, but again fell into the hands of the English in 1794. Previous to the union of Corsica with France, B. was the cap. of the island. Pop. in 1787, 7,916; in 1821, 9,316; in 1831, 9,531; in 1836, 13,061; and in 1841, 14,568.
BASTIA, a town of the duchy of Modena, on a tributary of the Panaro, 7 m. NE of Modena.
BASTIA, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the del. and 11 m. ESE of Perugia.

BASTIDE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Carbon-Blanc, and com. of Cenon-La-Bastide. Pop. 900.-Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, and cant. of Boulon. Pop. 1,022.

BASTIDE (LA), or LA BASTIDE-FORTONIERE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, arrond. of Gourdon. The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 7,158. The village is 12 m. SE of Gourdon, and contains 1,420 inhabitants.
BASTIDE - D'ARMAGNAC (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gers, and cant. of Cazaubon, situated on the l. bank of the Douze. Pop. 1,722.
BASTIDE-DE-BEARN (LA), or LA-BASTIDEDE-VILLEFRANCHE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, and cant. of Salies. Pop. 905.

BASTIDE-CLAIRENCE (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Basses- Pyrénées. The cant. comprises 4 com., and contained in 1831, 7,164 inhabitants. The town is situated near the r. bank of the Joyeuse, 13 m. ESE of Bayonne, and contains extensive manufactories of hosiery, and tanneries. In the environs is a mine of yellow copper, and one of iron called Usteley mine.

BASTIDE-CLERMONT (LA), or LA BASTIDEDES-FEUILLANTS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, and cant. of Rieumes, 14 m. SW of Muret. It formerly contained an abbey of the order of Citeaux, founded in 1162. Pop. 804.
BASTIDE-L'EVEQUE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, and cant. of Rieupeyroux. It has some paper-mills. Pop. 2,899.

BASTIDE-SUR-L'HERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ariege, and cant. of Mirepoix, 6 m. NE of Pamiers. It possesses manufactories of jet, which is found in the locality, and of combs. To the S of the village is a tepid mineral spring.
BASTIDE-DES-JOURDANS (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, and cant. of Pertuis, 15 m. ESE of Apt. In the vicinity is a flint quarry. Pop. 1,000.
BASTIDE-DE-LEVIS (LA), or LA BASTIDEMONTFORT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Tarn, in the cant. and 6 m. NE of Gaillac.
BASTIDE-ROUAIROUX (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn, and cant. of St. Amans-la-Bastide. It contains some cloth manufactories. Pop. 2,412.

BASTIDE-DE-SERON (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ariege, and arrond. of Foix. The cant. comprises 12 com. and contained in 1831, 8,375 inhabitants. The town is situated near the source of the Arize, 10 m. NW of Foix. Grey and red clay are found in the environs. The former is extensively used in the manufacture of crucibles for the glass-works of Arbas, Pointis, and St. Croix. Some of the streams in the vicinity contain particles of gold. Pop. 2,911.
BASTIE. See BATIE.
BASTIGLIA, a town of the duchy of Modena, 15 m. SSW of the capital.
BASTIL (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, and cant. of Gramar. Pop. 1,142.

BASTIMENTOS, a port on the N coast of the isthmus of Panama, to the NE of Porto Bello, in N lat. 10° 10', and E long. 79° 40'. It is formed by a line of rocky islands of the same name—which stretch along the coast at the distance of about 500 paces— and affords excellent anchorage. Two of the islands are of considerable dimensions, and produce good timber, but they are all uninhabited.

BASTION, or Bastion de France, a small village of Algeria, in the prov. of Constantina, near La Cala. In the vicinity is the bastion built in 1520, at the period of the first establishment of a French company in Africa.
BASTOE, an island of Norway, in Christianiafiord, in N lat. 59° 22' 35", E long. 10° 36'. There is a fixed light upon it, 29 ft. above high water.
BASTOGNE, the ancient Belsonacum, a town of Belgium, cap. of an administrative arrond., in the prov. and 32 m. NW of Luxemburg, and 23 m. NNW of Arlon, on the Wilz. Pop. 2,111.
BASTON, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. NW of Market-Deeping. Area 3,520 acres. Pop. 765.
BASUNHAY. See BACACAY.
BASVILLE, or BAVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. and 3 m. NE of Crocq. Pop. 957.
BASWICK, a parish and town in the co. of Staf- ago, near the SE coast of the island of Luzon, at ford, 2 m. SE of Stafford. Area of p. 6,410 acres. Pop. 1,438.

BASZKID MOUNTAINS, a ramification of the Carpathian chain in Hungary, extending SW from 21° E long. It encloses the basin of the Poprad on the SE. The principal rivers which take their rise on its eastern side are the Topoly and Tarcza.
BATA, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Tolna, BAS on the r. bank of the Danube, at the confluence of the Sarviz, 36 m. E of Funfkirchen.
BATACAO, a town of New Granada, in the prov. and 21 m. N of Pamplona. It is pleasantly situated in a mountainous district, and possesses a temperate climate.
BATAC, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Philippine group, near the N coast of the island of Samar, in N lat. 12° 41', and E long. 124° 55'.
BATAGHI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the gov. of Roumelia, and sanjak of Gallipoli, 50 m. SW of Philoppopolis.
BATAL, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the gov. of Room, and sanj. of Buzok, on the l. bank of the Konah, 80 m. SW of Tokat.

BATALHA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, near the r. bank of the Lis, 7 m. SSW of Leiria. It is chiefly remarkable for a Dominican convent and church, built by John I. in commemoration of the victory of Aljubarrota in 1385. They are beautiful specimens of Normanno-Gothic architecture; and in the latter is the rich and mystically adorned mausoleum of the founder. The environs contain some salt springs. Pop. 1,600.

BATALIN, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Philippine group, to the SW of Cape Talyabo, on the E coast of the island of Celebes, in N lat. 1° 15', and E long. 123° 33'. Its length is 24 m. and its average breadth 7 m. It rises to a considerable height, and appears well-wooded.

BATANG, or BATTAM, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, at the southern extremity of the Malay peninsula, to the W of the island of Bintang, in N lat. 1° 10′, and E long. 103° 55′. It is 25 m. in length and 10 m. in average breadth, and is surrounded by numerous rocky islets.

BATANG, or Patang Hook, a fortress and port on the E coast of the island of Gilolo, in the Asiatic archipelago. The fortress, which is a natural excavation in a lofty rocky headland, is of great extent, and is only accessible by ladders. The summit of the rock presents a platform 3 m. in circumference, covered with gardens, olive plantations, and houses.

BATANGAS, a province and town of the island of Luzon, the largest of the Philippine group, in the Asiatic archipelago. The prov. is bounded on the N by the prov. of Cavite; on the NE by that of Laguna; on the E by the prov. of Tayabas; and on the S and SW by the Chinese sea. It contains several lofty mountains, and several extinct volcanoes, called the Seven Brothers, the craters of which are filled with water impregnated with salt and saltpetre. The district is in its greater extent remarkably fertile, producing indigo, cinnamon, nutmegs, cocoa, coffee, sugar, &c., and is generally well wooded. Cattle and wild buffaloes abound in all its parts. Nankin of various colours, cotton fabrics, and handkerchiefs are extensively manufactured by the inhabitants. The prov. contains 12 villages. - The capital, of the same name, is situated on a bay at the SW extremity of the island, 55 m. S of Manilla, in N lat. 13° 42′, and E long. 121° 10′. Pop. of the district in 1810, 131,917, of whom 3,997 were of mixed descent.
BATAVANAN, an island of the Asiatic archipelthe E point of the entrance of the bay of St. Miguel, in N lat. 14° 5'. and E long. 123° 23'.

BATAVIA, a district or residency of Java, bounded on the N by the sea of Java; on the E by the district of Crawang; on the S by Buitenzorg; and on the W by Bantam. It is about 60 m. in length from E to W, and 20 m. from N to S; and is divided into 4 departments. The principal productions are cotton, pepper, and coffee. The pop. in | 1821 was 182,654.

BATAVIA, the capital of the above district, and of the Dutch settlements in the East Indies, is situated on the N coast of the island of Java, at the mouth of the Jacatra, in S lat. 6° 9' 20", E long. 106° 51' 45", 60 m. ESE of the straits of Sunda. It lies in the bosom of a large and commodious bay, which is so sheltered from the violence of the wind, by a number of low green islands scattered along its mouth, that it is considered one of the safest harbours in the world, and it is so capacious that a thousand sail of ships may ride there in safety. The city was surrounded with a broad wall fortified by 22 bastions, and a deep moat which can be filled at pleasure with water from the river; but the walls were removed in 1811. All the avenues on the land-side are protected by forts and redoubts erected at a considerable distance from the town, mounted with brass cannon, and built entirely of dressed stones. The approach by sea is commanded by a horn-work commonly called the Water-fort, mounting 14 guns and 2 howitzers; and the entrance of the river is defended by the citadel, and several batteries of 6 or 7 guns each. There is also a fortification upon Onroost, one of the islands in the mouth of the bay, which completely commands the channel forming the principal passage into the road. Upon this island the Dutch have established extensive dock-yards, where everything necessary for building, equipping, and preserving the company's ships, is provided in such abundance, that they can build, repair, and refit their vessels, without the least loss of time, and in the most complete manner. The citadel, situated on the E bank of the river, is a regular square fortification, built of coral rock, and flanked with 4 bastions. It contains the palace of the governor-general of the Indies, and the apartments of the councillors and other principal officers of the company. The most formidable fortifications of B., however, are to be found in the surrounding swamps and marshes, which can only be traversed over a few roads, the destruction of which would render the approach of artillery impracticable. The arsenals and magazines are generally well-furnished, and always contain stores and ammunition sufficient for a regular siege. The city itself,—though it has been highly admired for the beauty of its buildings,contains nothing of elegance or design particularly worthy of notice. In the old town there is but one church remaining, the Lutheran, a large and wellbuilt edifice. The house occupied by the Netherlands Commercial company is also a fine edifice. The streets are straight and regular, and overshadowed and embellished with large trees which are planted on each side; but of the magnificence which procured for this capital the title of 'Queen of the East,' little is now to be found: streets have been pulled down,-canals half-filled up,-forts demolished,—and palaces levelled with the dust. The first appearance of B., when you have fairly entered into the town-for before then, it is, in common with the native towns, hid in a forest of ever-verdant fruit and ornamental trees-is indeed rather imposing; but the houses, in the European parts of the town, though spacious, are inelegant, and built according to no known rules of art. The upper story is usually a receptacle for lumber; and the lower, or ground-floor, is filled with a quantity of clumsy furniture, such as cabinet work,—the ill-finished manufacture of the country, after the Dutch models of the 16th cent., -lustres of painted glass, and defaced mirrors. The recently extended connection with Europe is gradually dispelling this kind of barbarism, and the modern settlers successfully imitate the taste and fashions of the British. The general outline of the city is an oblong square, about three-quarters of a mile long, and half-a-mile broad, intersected by the river Jacatra, which runs through the middle of it, from S to N, and which is crossed by three bridges. In its passage, this river forms fifteen canals, which are faced with freestone, while their banks are adorned with evergreen shrubs and plants. The breadth of the river within the walls is from 160 to 180 ft. At its mouth are two large piers of wood and brick work, running about halfa-mile into the sea, between which gangs of slaves are constantly employed in clearing out the mud which is washed from the town, and which otherwise would choke up the channel of the river. The suburbs are very extensive and populous; and are inhabited chiefly by Chinese, and natives of the island. The city, as seen from the sea, appears almost sunk in the flat beech, only showing its roofs; and the environs, to a considerable extent, present one universal garden, intersected with rivulets and canals, overshadowed with lofty trees, and interspersed with magnificent villas. The Dutch have here indulged their national taste to its full extent: every spot is covered with verdure and luxuriant foliage. - The few public institutions are: the orphan-chamber, which administers to the estates of all persons dying intestate, or whose executors are absent,—the supreme college-of-justice, consisting of a president and two members,—and a literary society, instituted in 1777, and renewed during the temporary government of the British. This society has published several volumes of transactions, which contain a few essays of some merit. The administration of the town and the management of the police are solely in the hands of government, who depute their authority to a bench of magistrates.—B. is even better known in Europe by its fatal climate, than by its great trade and central situation. The disease which chiefly proves mortal, is a fever of the remittent kind. Dysenteries are very rare; and inflammations of the liver, which terminate fatally by the formation of matter, are of a chronic nature, and almost always the consequence of long-continued spirituous intemperance. The merchants who transact business in the town during the day enjoy as large a share of health as the European residents of any tropical climate whatever: but a stranger who sleeps for six or eight days successively in the town, may certainly reckon on catching the fever, and it is more than an equal chance but he falls a victim to this terrible malady. This extreme insalubrity is in great part to be attributed to the recession of the sea for a space of many hundred yards, by which an extensive mud flat is left uncovered, and to the injudicious dissipation of the waters of the river into numerous stagnant canals, which poison the purity of the air, and deprive the river of the natural impetus which would have kept its channel clear of the impurities that now choke its mouth, or lie putrifying on its banks. [Quart. Journ. of Science, vol. vii.] At present the salubrity of the site has enticed all respectable persons to take up their residence at Weltevreden, Konigsplein, or Meister Cornelius, about 6 m. beyond the city, where you may ride for several miles amidst elegant countryseats built in the English or Italian style. Few Europeans, indeed, reside in B., excepting those who are directly concerned in the shipping. The summerrange of the therm. is from 70° to 74° in the mornings and evenings, and 80° at noon.-Of a pop. amounting in 1815 to 47,417, and in 1842 to 53,861, scarcely 3,000 are Europeans; and of these, not one-fifth are Dutch, the greater number being Portuguese and French; the rest are composed of a great variety of Indians, all under chiefs of their own nation, such as Chinese, Javanese, natives of the island, Malays, Amboynese, Mardykers, Balis, Bugis, Macassars, &c. Of all these, the Chinese are the most indefatigably industrious. They keep all the shops, and most of the inns in the city, and are in general the farmers of the duties, excises, and customs. The Javanese apply themselves chiefly to agriculture and ship-building; and the Malays to fishing .- All the dominions which the Dutch possess in the East are governed by two supreme councils, -the councilof-the-Indies, and the council-of-justice, both of which are fixed at B. To the first of these belongs the entire direction of public affairs; and to the latter, the administration of justice in all its branches. But the citizens and free-merchants are amenable only to the tribunal of the city of B.; which is composed of 8 aldermen, and a president, who is always a councillor of the Indies. The governor-general, who presides in the council of the Indies, possesses unlimited authority; is allowed a court, and most of the honours of majesty; and so great are the legal emoluments of his office, that without oppressing the people, or burdening his conscience, he is generally able to realize an immense fortune in a few years. He is, however, removable at the pleasure of the directors at home. The ecclesiastical government of B. resides in eleven members of the reformed religion. Liberty of conscience is granted to every denomination of religionists, though not liberty of worship: Mahommedans and Pagans are tolerated by government; and even the Chinese have their temples, in which they worship the Devil under the figure of a gigantic colossus, sitting crosslegged with an enormous belly hanging over his knees; but the exercise of the Roman Catholic religion is obstinately prohibited. The regular establishment of troops at this colony seldom exceeds 3,000 men, of which 700 only are Europeans; yet few of these are furnished by the Dutch; they are chiefly Germans, many of whom, it is said, have been kidnapped into the service. The irregulars are numerous, consisting of Chinese and natives of the island, who are commanded by their own officers. All the white inhabitants, however, are trained to the use of arms; for no person can settle here but as a soldier in the company's service; and he must serve a certain time before he is allowed to enter into any branch of trade. The bay and harbour of B. are excellently adapted for commercial navigation. The trade consists chiefly in the valuable productions of the island, such as pepper, rice, sugar, coffee, and indigo; and as B. is the emporium where all the merchandise of the Dutch company in India is deposited, the import and export duties are very considerable. The chief imports are opium and piece-goods. The commerce of B. is that of the Netherlands Commercial company, and that of a number of Dutch, English and American mercantile houses. The number of trading vessels belonging to the port is between 40 and 50, including one or two steam-boats. The manufacturing establishments here are chiefly distilleries of arrack, brick-kilns, tanneries, dye-works, and potteries. The establishment of this colony upon the ruins of a royal city: in opposition to the efforts of the English, and afterwards to the united forces of the populous empire of Java, and its subsequent prosperity, affords a striking example of what may be effected by courage and perseverance. When the Dutch first visited this island, the residence of the king of Jacatra was an inconsiderable village feebly pallisadoed with bamboo-canes. Having entered into an alliance with that prince, they contracted with him for the produce of his little territory, which consisted chiefly of pepper; but afterwards, suspecting him of a breach of faith, they built a strong fort in the neighbourhood to awe him into justice. This raised the jealousy of the English, who had also some correspondence with the people of Jacatra. The fleets of the two nations engaged at a short distance from the fort, when the Dutch were completely defeated. The English then took possession of Ι. Jacatra, and, upon an eminence in the middle of the town, established a magazine which they fortified with a considerable number of heavy cannon. Vanden Broecke, the Dutch commander of Fort Maurice, hard pressed for ammunition and provisions, threw himself on the protection of the governor of Bantam, who immediately despatched 2,000 men to his assistance. The Bantamese entered Jacatra, stripped the native king of all ensigns of royalty, and drove him with his family, helpless and poor, to a distant corner of the island, where he dragged out his existence in the humble condition of a fisherman. Peace being soon after concluded between the two companies, the English retired from the island, leaving the Dutch in possession of Jacatra, but so completely under the power of the Bantamese, that Vanden Broecke and seventy of his men were carried prisoners to Bantam. This unfavourable aspect of affairs, however, was soon changed by the arrival of a Dutch squadron. Commodore Koen, who after his defeat had retired to Amboyna, having received a strong reinforcement, returned in 1619 with a fleet of 17 sail and a considerable body of troops; and having ravaged and destroyed the town of Jacatra, marched upon Bantam, and demanded and obtained the restitution of Vanden Broecke and his companions. Koen returned immediately to Fort Maurice; and, upon the ruins of Jacatra, in the midst of fens and morasses, and under a vertical sun, he laid the foundation of the city of B. National taste, and national prejudices, seem to have dictated the choice of this situation; the plan, however, was so well contrived, and, notwithstanding local disadvantages, the execution was so prompt and successful, that B. speedily became, and has since continued, the cap. of the Dutch conquests and settlements in the East. The emperor of Java, who had beheld with satisfaction the jealousy between the Dutch and English, now began to take alarm at the growing prosperity and gradual encroachments of his ambitious neighbours. He first attempted to take off governor Koen by assassination; but that design failing, he drew together an immense army, and determined to extirpate the intruders. In the beginning of 1629, B. was invested by 200,000 Javanese; but the Dutch works were defended with such courage and conduct, that, after a siege of several months, the assailants were obliged to retire broken and discomfited. The good fortune of the colony in this enterprise, however, was greatly overbalanced by the death of the governor-general, to whose wisdom and activity it had owed its existence and preservation. Soon after these events, the governor of Bantam, having revolted from the emperor of Java, assumed the title of king, and was supported in the quality of an independent prince by the government of B. Two separate interests were thus formed in the empire; and the Dutch, by dexterously playing the one against the other, were enabled not only to maintain their own power, but considerably to extend their territory. Not contented, however, with a precarious security which the united efforts of the two rivals might annihilate, they contrived a scheme to get into their hands the persons of the two rival chiefs, which was prosecuted by means the most deceitful and unjustifiable, and in the space of a few years was accomplished. All fears of foreign hostility were now allayed, and the Dutch began to indulge the hope of domestic peace and settled security; but the natives, far from being reconciled to their authority, made repeated efforts to drive them from the island, and to restore their sovereigns to independence. The avarice and injustice of the Dutch had sown the seeds of disaffection, and at a time when seeming tranquillity reigned around, they were upon the very brink of destruction, and thousands waited only the signal to take vengeance on their oppressors. A conspiracy of a most daring and dangerous nature had been for four years forming in the very heart of the settlement; to surprise the city, and put every European and Christian to the sword. The execution of this purpose was fixed for the first morning of 1722; the order of attack was prepared and delivered to the chiefs of the conspiracy; 17,000 men were engaged to insure its accomplishment, and waited only the signal for striking the final blow; when, on the day preceding this intended massacre, the conspiracy was divulged, just in time to prevent the dreadful catastrophe. Twenty of the principal conspirators were secretly seized, and all necessary precautions taken in case of any commotion in the city; but the confederates were so thunderstruck at the discovery that not the least attempt was made for their rescue. Erberfeldt and Catadia, the principal conspirators, were condemned "to be extended and bound each of them upon a cross, to have their right hands cut off, and their arms, legs, and breasts pinched with red hot pincers, till pieces of the flesh should be torn away. Their bellies were then to be ripped from bottom to top, and their hearts thrown in their faces; after which their heads were to be cut off, and fixed upon a post; and their bodies, torn in pieces, were to be exposed to the fowls of the air without the city, in whatever place the government should please to direct." [Sentence contre Pierre Erberfeldt, et ses complices, prononcée a Batavia en 1722.]-This terrible sentence was executed without the least mitigation. Its severity, however, had not the desired effect; and eighteen years after the conspiracy of Erberfeldt, the Dutch proceeding upon mere supposition, committed one of the most inhuman massacres that has ever disgraced the annals of any nation. On the memorable morning of the 9th of October, 1740, an order was issued by the governor and council for immediately putting to the sword all the Chinese that could be found in the city. The garrison, and the sailors who were brought from the vessels in the roads, were tempted by the promise of plunder to execute the bloody edict. The houses were broken open, and the sleeping victims were torn from their beds and killed without distinction. According to the acknowledgment of the Dutch themselves, no fewer than 12,000 Chinese, but according to other accounts, 30,000 men, women, and children, were thus butchered. It must be added, for the honour of humanity, that the governor was seized on his way home, and carried back to B., to abide a trial for the shameful abuse of his authority; and that he remained a prisoner until his death. The Chinese were invited by a general amnesty to return to the settlement, and in a short time became as numerous as ever; and order and tranquillity having been restored by the firm and wise administration of the new governor, the inhabitants soon lost all apprehensions of any future disturbance. In 1811, B. was taken by a British force under Sir Samuel Achmuty; but it was restored to the Dutch again in August 1816.—Stavorinus.— Staunton.—Raffles.—Horsburgh.—Hogendorp's Coup d'Œil.

BATAVIA, a township and village of New York, U. S., in Genesee co. The former presents a level surface, and is watered by the Tonawanda creek. Its soil, compounded of sand and calcareous loam, is generally fertile. Pop. 4,219. The village is connected by the Tonawanda railroad with the Great Western. It was incorporated in 1823, and consists of 300 houses. Pop. in 1840, 2,000.—Also a village of Ohio, cap. of Clermont co., situated on the N bank of the E fork of the Little Miami, 103 m. SW of Columbus. Pop. in 1840, 2,187.—Also a township of Ohio, U. S., in Geaugu co., intersected by branches of the Cuyahoga and Grand rivers. Pop. in 1840, 771.—Also a township of Michigan, in Branch co. Pop. in 1840, 400.
BATAVIA, a river of North Australia, which falls into the eastern side of the gulf of Carpentaria, in S lat. 11° 50′, 90 m. SSW of Cape York. The length of its course is unknown.
BATCAB, a town of Yucatan, to the E of the lake of the same name, and 130 m. SE of Campeche.
BATCHEEWAUAUNG, or BATCHWING, a town of Upper Canada, situated on a bay of the same name, on the E side of Lake Superior, 50 m. S of Michipicoton.
BATCHI, a town of Macedonia, near the l. bank of the Tzerna, 17 m. ESE of Monastir.

BATCHIAN, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Molucca group, separated from the SW coast of the island of Gilolo by the strait of Patientia, and situated within the 1st degree of S. lat. It is 52 m. in length, and 20 in average breadth, and is generally mountainous. Its principal agricultural productions are sago and grain. Gold in considerable quantities is found in some localities. The surrounding sea abounds with fish. The inhabitants are chiefly Malay Mahommedans, and are governed by a chief subordinate to the Dutch, who formerly claimed the sovereignty of the adjacent islands of Ooby, Ceram, and Goram. The Spaniards were expelled from this island by the Dutch in 1610.
BATCOMBE, a parish in Dorsetshire, 9 m. S of Sherbourne. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 171.—Also a p. in Somersetshire, 3 m. N of Braton. Area 3,150 acres. Pop. 780.
BATCULL. See BATTECOLLAH.

BATE, or Bhatta, an island of Hindostan, at the SW extremity of the gulf of Cutch, in N lat. 22° 25', E long. 69° 20'. It is barren, and incapable of sustaining its pop., said to be 7,000 in number; but it possesses a good harbour.
BATEAH, a town of Yucatan, 190 m. SSW of Merida.—Also a town of Spain, in the prov. of Tarragona, 20 m. NW of Tortosa.
BATEELI, or RATEA, a town in the island of Borneo, on the S coast, on the estuary of the Banjarmassin river, in S lat 3° 20'.
BATELES, a river of La Plata, in the prov. of Entre Rios, flowing SSW into the Parana, on the I. bank, 60 m. below St. Lucia.
BATEMAN BAY, a small gulf on the SE coast of New South Wales, to the S of Cape Upright, in the co. of St. Vincent, into which the rivers Clyde and Macleay discharge themselves. BAT
BATENBURG, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gueldres, and arrond. of Nimeguen, on the r. bank of the Meuse. Pop. 600. It is supposed to be on the site of the ancient Oppidum Batavorum.

BATES, a county in the state of Missouri, U. S., watered by the Osage river and its branches. It is a surface of 1,160 sq. m., chiefly prairie land. Its temporary cap. is Batesville, on the N bank of the Osage river.

BATESVILLE, a town of Arkansas, U.S., the cap. of Independence co., 90 m. NNE of Little Rock. —Also a village in Haversham co., Georgia, 163 m. N by W of Milledgeville .- Also the cap. of Bates co., Missouri.—Also a township in Guernsey co., Ohio.

BATH, a handsome and interesting city, in the hund. of Bath-Forum, at the NE extremity of Somersetshire, near the borders of Wilts and Gloucestershire. It stands in a narrow valley intersected by the Avon, and is surrounded by an amphitheatre of hills of considerable elevation, belonging to the great western oolitic range. The river Avon-which is navigable from hence to Bristol-winds for about 2 m. through the city and its suburbs; and a direct communication with London, through the cos. of Middlesex, Bucks, Berks, Hampshire, and Wilts, is afforded by the Kennet and Avon canal, which here joins the Avon, and is connected with the Thames at Reading; and by the Great Western railway, which connects Bath with London on the one hand, and Bristol on the other.
