The city is on the direct road from London to Bristol; and is distant from the former, by way of Maidenhead and Calne, 107 m., or by railway 1063 m.; from Chippenham, 12 m., or by railway 13 m.; from Wells 19 m.; and from Weston-super-Mare, by railway, 30 m. Its geog. position is N lat. 51° 23', W long. 2° 22'. Mean temp. in June, 61.20; in August, 62.70. B., with Wells, forms a bishopric, the revenues of which average yearly the gross sum of £6,770. The dio. extends over the whole co. of Somerset, with the exception of a small portion of the city of Bristol. The episcopal palace is at Wells. The Abbeychurch is a fine building, nearly coeval with the chapels of King's college, Cambridge, and Henry Seventh's chapel, Westminster; but though it possesses the general features of the style in which these edifices are built, it is itself simple and unadorned even to plainness. It is in the form of a cross, from the intersection of which rises a quadrangular tower to the height of 170 ft. The length is 210 ft., by 72 ft. in breadth, and 78 ft. in height. The interior is remarkably light and elegant, and crowded with ornaments. B. likewise contains 26 parish-churches and Episcopal chapels, 5 Wesleyan and 4 Baptist chapels, and places of worship for Roman Catholics, Independents, Unitarians, and other sects. —The Circus is a noble range of buildings, in which the fronts of the houses are embellished with a series of double pillars of the Doric, Ionic, and Corinthian orders, rising one above another, and supporting a battlement decorated with sculpture. Not inferior in elegance are the houses which compose the Royal crescent, and command a fine view of the city, the vale of the Avon, and the distant hills. Behind the Royal crescent rise rows of houses one above another, with a progressive elevation unusually favourable to the display of architectural beauties. On the E side of the city is Pulteney bridge, an elegant structure of 3 arches covered on both sides with shops, and leading from High street, in the centre of the city, to Bathwick. On this side also of the town is Laura-place, a handsome square, intersected diagonally by Great Pulteney street. At the extremity of this street is the Pulteney hotel, an elegant erection, behind which lie the Sidney gardens, an extensive piece of ground forming an area of over 20 acres, laid out in the style of the London Vauxhall. The Kennet and Avon canal passes through these grounds, over which are thrown two cast-iron bridges in the Chinese style. They likewise contain the Gothic hall, a fanciful and pretty erection, raised in 1845, which is capable of containing 1,000 persons, and is used for balls, concerts, lectures, &c. Behind Sidney formerly much resorted to as a fashionable promenade prospects are very pleasing. Among the public buildings of B. may be mentioned the Guild-hall, an elegant structure, with a portico of 4 Corinthian columns, and supporting a triangular pediment with a rich entablature and cornice. Under the new municipal act, B. is divided into 7 wards, and is governed by a mayor, 14 aldermen, and 42 councillors. B., including the old city, and the parishes of Bathwick, Lyncombe and Widcombe, and Walcot, returns two members to parliament. The elective franchise was first conferred in the reign of Edward I. The right of election was formerly vested in the mayor, aldermen, and common-council. The number of electors on the register for 1837 was 3,095; for 1842-3, 2,955; for 1848-9, 3,074.—The pop. of the city in 1801 was 34,160; in 1811, 38,434; in 1821, 46,688; in 1831, 50,800; and in 1841, 52,346. The income of the borough, including borough-rates, in 1849, was £22,516; expenditure £22,533. The city bond-debt in 1849 was 67,400. The police force of the city comprises a chief officer, 5 inspectors, 10 sergeants, a clerk, and 70 constables.—The trade of B. is in a great measure dependent on its visitors. There are constantly many strangers, from all parts of the United kingdom, the West Indies, and the Continent, resident here; so much so, that in full season there is an addition to the permanent pop. of 14,000 persons. The manufacture of coarse woollen cloth, called Bath-coating, was at one time carried on here and in the neighbourhood to a considerable extent, but it has long ceased to exist. Some of the finer descriptions of West of England broad-cloths are still manufactured in the neighbourhood. The baths, to which this city owes its name, and in a great measure its prosperity, are 5 in number; the King's bath, the Queen's bath, the Hot bath, the Cross bath, and the Kingston bath, which last is the property of Earl Manvers, but is now opened to the public, on three days in the week, at charges which are almost nominal. The public and private baths are different branches of the same establishment, the exclusive property—with the above exception—of the corporation. The grand Pump-room is the finest hall connected with the baths. It is a handsome erection, 85 ft. in length, 48 ft. in breadth, and 34 ft. in height, adorned with columns of the Corinthian order, supporting a rich entablature and a lofty ceiling, and lighted by a double range of windows.—The King's bath is a large basin, 60 ft. 11 in. in length, and 40 ft. 2 in. in breadth; containing, when filled to the ordinary level, 314 tons of water. It is supplied with water directly from the spring, which rises in the centre with great force—at the rate of 128 gallons per minute. Temp. 115°.—The Queen's bath is attached to the King's, and opens into it. Its temp. is somewhat lower.--The Cross bath, at the W extremity of Bath-street, about 150 yards from the King's bath, yields 12 gall. per minute, of the temp. of 109°.-The Hot bath, so denominated from its superior degree of heat, which ranges about 117°, is 40 yards to the SW of the King's bath, and is furnished with dry and vapour baths, to which a small pump-room has been added.-The Kingston baths, which belong to the Earl of Manvers, are provided with sudatories and various other conveniences. - By chemical analysis, the B. waters have been found to contain carbonic acid and nitrogen gas in small quantity, some sulphate and muriate of soda, sulphate and carbonate of lime, silicious earth, and oxide of iron. Their taste is by no means unpleasant, and the use of them has been recommended in gout, rheumatism, paralytic affections, biliary obstructions, and cutaneous disorders. The general indications for the use of this medicinal water are in cases where a gentle stimulus is required. The cases to which it is more particularly suited are mostly of the chronic kind. The reader who wishes a more ela- ~ borate account and analysis of the hot springs at Bath is recommended to consult Mr. Richard Philip's paper on the subject in No. 96 of the Philosophical Magazine, or an elaborate work just issued, from the pen of Dr. Tunstall, resident medical officer of the Bath hospital, termed The Bath Waters, their uses and effects. Mr. M. Noad, lecturer on chemistry at St. George's hospital, has recently made some very minute experiments upon the B. waters; and he thus states the composition of an imperial quart:- Chloride of calcium, 5.4500 grains. Chloride of magnesium, The public amusements in B. are conducted on a liberal scale. The most important of these are the subscription assemblies and balls, which take place during the season at the Upper Assembly rooms, a spacious and elegant pile of buildings erected in 1791. The theatre is a handsome edifice, fitted up in a splendid style with three tiers of boxes; the roof is divided into compartments, and till recently contained the beautiful paintings by Cassali, which formerly occupied a similar situation in Fonthill abbev. In 1830, a handsome park, named after Queen Victoria, was established near the Royal crescent; it stands upon an area of about 12 acres, is laid out with beautiful walks and rides, and is adorned by a tasteful obelisk called the Victoria column. In the vicinity of the town, especially on Lansdown, there are delightful spots for equestrian exercise. Races take place on Lansdown in April, and a fair is held there for horses, cattle, and general merchandise, on the 10th of August. B. has also three other fairs held on the 14th Feb., 14th May, and 10th July. The influx of visitors now takes place about Christmas, and the "season" lasts till the end of May or beginning of June. Formerly it was much earlier. The Bath Literary and Scientific institution was established in 1820. The Bath and West of England society for the encouragement of agriculture, the arts, manufactures, and commerce, by the distribution of rewards and medals, was established in 1777. An Athenæum exists here, a Commercial and Literary society, and circulating libraries are numerous. The subscription club possesses a fine club-house in York buildings. It was established in 1790. The members are elected by ballot; but the members of the London west-end clubs are eligible without ballot. -The public charities are numerous and well-administered. The B. hospital, founded in 1742, is an establishment incorporated by act of parliament, and open to poor persons from all parts of the kingdom, who may be suffering under diseases capable of being benefited by the use of the Bath waters. It contains 133 beds, and is supported by subscription. The United hospital, founded by the junction of the Bath city infirmary and the Bath casualty hospital, in 1826, is a spacious building near the Cross-bath. A valuable anatomical museum is attached to this hospital. The annual revenue of the hospital of St. John the Baptist is somewhere about £1,000 per ann. There is a Central National school here, several endowed and free schools, and numerous private educational establishments. A splendid Roman Catholic collegiate establishment has been erected at Prior park. Charitable institutions abound in this city and neighbourhood; and among the most noble of them is a college on Newbridge hill, founded by a lady named Partis. It is for the support of 30 decayed gentlewomen: each of whom has a separate house and garden, and receives an annual allowance. The origin of B. is involved in obscurity. Its name, and in all probability its original importance, were derived from its medicinal springs. There is no evidence to prove that the virtues of the B. waters were known to the inhabitants of the district prior to the Roman invasion; although some have maintained that the Britons had a town here called Caer Badon, or 'the place of Baths.' Be this as it may, it seems beyond a doubt that the town was enlarged, and baths erected here, in the reign of Claudius. The site of these baths was discovered in 1755, on removing the foundations of the old abbey house, or priory, at the depth of from 11 to 20 ft. below the surface of the ground. Bath soon became a favourite resort of the Romans, who subsequently bestowed upon it the name of Aqua Solis; it was also the Fontis Calidæ of Ptolemy and Antoninus. The Saxons called it Hat Baden, or 'hot baths,' and Acemannes-ceaster, or 'the city of sick men.' In 1377, B. contained only 579 lay-inhabitants. In the early part of the civil wars, it was fortified for the king; but on the retreat of Hertford into Wales, it was taken by the army of the parliament, and became the head-quarters of the army raised by General Waller. The battle of Lansdown took place in the vicinity in 1643; but the city was ultimately surrendered to parliament in 1645. The most interesting points connected with its subsequent history are those which mark the various changes and improvements which it has successively undergone. The project of extending the city, and embellishing it with handsome streets, was first suggested about the year 1728 by an enterprising architect, whose plans were facilitated by the abundance of excellent freestone which the neighbouring hills supplied. B. is now one of the handsomest cities in England. To its natural advantages of situation it owes not a little; but the resources of art have also been liberally expended upon it.- The principal historian of Bath is the Rev. Richard Warner, whose work was published in 1801, in 1 vol. 4to.—Among the celebrated men who were either born in, or adopted the city for residence, were John Palmer, the inventor of mail-coaches, and the renowned wit "Beau" Nash.

BATH, a sea-port in Lincoln co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 37 m. S of Augusta, on the W bank of the Kennebec, 12 m. from the ocean. Pop. in 1801, 2,491; in 1840, 5,141. It is one of the principal commercial towns in Maine. The tonnage of the port in 1840 amounted to 64,035 tons. Steam-boats ply three-fourths of the year between B. and Portland and Boston. Ship-building is an extensive business here.—Also a township in Grafton co., New Hampshire, on the E bank of the Connecticut. Pop. 1,595.—Also the cap. of Steuben co., in the state of New York, 219 m. WSW of Albany, on the N side of Conhocton river. Pop. 4,915.—Also a village on the E side of Hudson river, opposite Albany.—Also a township in Summit co., Ohio, 129 m. NE of Columbus.—Also a central co. of Virginia, on the headbranches of James, Cowpasture, and Jackson's rivers. Area 756 sq. m. Pop. 4,008.—Also a village in the · last-mentioned co., 164 m. WNW of Richmond, near which is a warm spring of a temp. of 96°, and another 6 m. to the SW of a temp. of 112°. - Also a county in the NE part of Kentucky, on Licking river. Pop. 9,763. Cap. Owingsville.—Also a township in Greene co., Ohio, watered by Mad river. Pop. 1,721.—Also a township in Van Diemen's land, on the N boundary of Monmouth county.
BATHA, a town and circle of Hungary, sometimes called Frauenmarkt, in the com. of Honth, 30 m. N of Gran, the property of Prince Esterhazy. The surrounding territory is covered with vineyards.
